text: One of my thoughts is that something like this would actually make a good programming tool for graphics programming. Parts of graphics programming are visual in many engines (shader programming for custom materials, VFX), but I actually think a lot more of it could be. Specifically graphics pipeline and resource binding code

text:
Hi <@UNGNT3BKR> - I liked reading your thesis. The problem is well described and also resonates deeply with me. Somehow I managed to miss this announcement and also your earlier post in feedback. Another write-up you might find interesting is <https://humane.computer/killing-apps/>.

An idea related to facets seems to be the datatypes in AmigaOS which were a system-wide registry of media formats: <http://www.mfischer.com/legacy/amosaic-paper/datatypes.html>, <https://wiki.amigaos.net/wiki/Datatypes_Library>

attachments.title: Killing Apps
attachments.text: An application program is a computer program designed to perform a group of coordinated functions, tasks, or activities for the benefit of the user. — Wikipedia Recently, I needed to create a presentation for work to introduce a lot of technical material to groups of people as efficiently as possible. Google

text: I think a lot of this is a case of path-dependence in the architecture. The notebooks we have now (IPython/Jupyter and descendents) have an architecture where the client-side notebook is basically a glorified interface to a REPL that runs on a backend with very little state shared between them. The simplicity makes it very easy to write new kernels or new frontends (nteract, VSCode, etc.) but it also means that those architectural choices persist. Some side effects of this architecture have just become accepted as how notebooks "work": tab-complete doesn't work when code is running; if you lose your network connection you lose output of running code; etc.

elements.text: I think a lot of this is a case of path-dependence in the architecture. The simplicity makes it very easy to write new kernels or new frontends (nteract, VSCode, etc.) but it also means that those architectural choices persist. Some side effects of this architecture have just become accepted as how notebooks "work": tab-complete doesn't work when code is running; if you lose your network connection you lose output of running code; etc.

The simplicity makes it very easy to write new kernels or new frontends (nteract, VSCode, etc.) but it also means that those architectural choices persist. Some side effects of this architecture have just become accepted as how notebooks "work": tab-complete doesn't work when code is running; if you lose your network connection you lose output of running code; etc.

text: I also think part of the problem is how languages like Python, R, and Julia have become the lingua Franca of data science, but all are too dynamic to support static type checking or static analysis of the dataflow graph which would make a whole lot more possible

text:
notebooks have always felt like a step backwards in a lot of ways to me: especially considering how the "great" recent innovation in structuring information is hypertext, and nonlinear presentation and exploration of information, while notebooks impose a linear narrative on you
I think part of it is the lack of time-reversibility in the languages in question—there's no easy "undo button"
In a lot of ways, a smalltalk style language with versioned image based code distribution focused on working with data could solve a lot of these issues

notebooks have always felt like a step backwards in a lot of ways to me: especially considering how the "great" recent innovation in structuring information is hypertext, and nonlinear presentation and exploration of information, while notebooks impose a linear narrative on you
I think part of it is the lack of time-reversibility in the languages in question—there's no easy "undo button"
In a lot of ways, a smalltalk style language with versioned image based code distribution focused on working with data could solve a lot of these issues

text: Additionally, the lack of decent crossplatform gui toolkits has forced any serious project involving graphics onto the web, and that limits the extent of how well the ux can be engineered and integrated, which is unfortunate.

text: <@USUMN6XLH> Just in case you haven't seen it, after Chris handed off Light Table to the community, he worked on a project called Eve that has other interesting ideas about juxtaposing the code and a live view of the data. Here's a nice overview of some of the prototypes they created: <http://futureofcoding.org/essays/eve/>
